blocks.body: Bare Infinitive is the base form of the verb without to after modal verbs and structures like let, make, and help.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"In this lesson you will fix the idea of the bare infinitive - the base form without to - and see where it appears most often: after modals, let, make, and sometimes help.","rules":[{"label":"WITHOUT TO","color":"emerald","text":"A bare infinitive = the base verb without the particle to.","example":"can swim / must leave / let him speak"},{"label":"AFTER MODALS","color":"blue","text":"After can, must, should, might, will, and other modal verbs, we use the bare infinitive.","example":"She can drive. / We must go."},{"label":"LET / MAKE / HELP","color":"amber","text":"In structures like let + object + verb and make + object + verb, there is no to; with help, both patterns are possible.","example":"They made us wait. / Help me carry this."}]}

{"title":"1. Main patterns","intro":"The bare infinitive has no to. Notice the most common structures.","items":[{"label":"After a modal verb","title":"modal + V","subtitle":"can go / should stay / must finish"},{"label":"Permission","title":"let + object + V","subtitle":"Let me explain. / They let us leave."},{"label":"Force","title":"make + object + V","subtitle":"The joke made us laugh."},{"label":"Help","title":"help + object + (to) V","subtitle":"Help me carry this. / Help me to carry this."}]}

{"title":"2. Where the bare infinitive appears","sections":[{"label":"After modal verbs","color":"emerald","description":"After modal verbs, we always use the form without to.","examples":[{"en":"You must wear a seat belt.","ua":"You must wear a seat belt."},{"en":"She can speak three languages.","ua":"She can speak three languages."},{"en":"We might arrive late.","ua":"We might arrive late."}]},{"label":"After let, make, and help","color":"blue","description":"In structures of permission, force, and help, the bare infinitive often comes after the object.","examples":[{"en":"My parents let me stay up late.","ua":"My parents let me stay up late."},{"en":"The teacher made us rewrite the paragraph.","ua":"The teacher made us rewrite the paragraph."},{"en":"Can you help me carry this box?","ua":"Can you help me carry this box?"}],"note":"With help, both help me carry and help me to carry are possible."},{"label":"Sometimes after verbs of perception","color":"amber","description":"After see, hear, watch, feel, the bare infinitive can show the whole action from start to finish.","examples":[{"en":"I saw him cross the street.","ua":"I saw him cross the street."},{"en":"We heard the baby cry.","ua":"We heard the baby cry."},{"en":"She watched them leave the building.","ua":"She watched them leave the building."}],"note":"The -ing form after perception verbs more often highlights the process rather than the full action."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Adding to after a modal verb.","wrong":"She can to drive.","right":"✅ She can drive.","hint":"After modal verbs, we use the bare infinitive without to."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using to after let.","wrong":"They let me to leave early.","right":"✅ They let me leave early.","hint":"The pattern with let is let + object + bare infinitive."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using to after make in the active structure.","wrong":"The film made us to cry.","right":"✅ The film made us cry.","hint":"In the active structure, make takes the bare infinitive."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["The bare infinitive = the base verb without to.","After modal verbs, we always use the bare infinitive: can go, must stop, should try.","After let and make, the form without to is also required.","With help, both patterns are possible, but the form without to is very common.","If you see a modal or a structure like let / make + object, check whether you added to by mistake."]}
